docs.title: webtasks
docs.text:
Browser-automation as a service. One Go binary, a folder of YAML tasks, and every task becomes a typed HTTP endpoint.

Ship selectors and Chrome internals on the server. Callers send JSON, get JSON back.

:octicons-rocket-24: Get started :octicons-play-24: Browse demos :octicons-code-24: Capy integration :octicons-mark-github-16: GitHub

docs.title: What is webtasks?
docs.text:
webtasks is a long-running browser-automation server built on chromedp (Chrome DevTools Protocol). You describe browser flows declaratively in YAML — goto, wait-for, click, extract, download-each, and dozens more — and the server exposes each task as POST /tasks/<name>.

Traditional stack webtasks JVM + chromedriver matched to Chrome Single ~17 MB Go binary Config baked into the jar Config bundle loaded at runtime (dir or zip) WebDriver download hacks Native CDP download control One-off scripts Reusable HTTP API with schemas

REST + SSE Hot-reload YAML Window pools PDF & screenshots GIF recording Network capture JS modules Secrets

docs.title: 30-second quick start
docs.text:
# 1. Build
go build -o build/webtasks ./cmd/webtasks

# 2. Start (demo bundle included in the repo)
WEBTASKS_BUNDLE=$(pwd)/demo ./build/webtasks &

# 3. Run a task
curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/tasks/basics/title \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{}' | python3 -m json.tool

Expected response:

{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "page": {
      "title": "Example Domain",
      "heading": "Example Domain",
      "body": "This domain is for use in documentation examples …"
    }
  }
}

Using the executor helper

If you have executor wired up locally:

executor build
executor server &
executor call basics/title
executor call crawl/hackernews-top

docs.title: How it fits together
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    Client["HTTP client\ncurl · Python · JS"]
    Server["webtasks\nGo server"]
    Bundle["Config bundle\ntasks/ · scripts/"]
    Chrome["Chrome pool\nchromedp"]
    Web["Target websites"]

    Client -->|"POST /tasks/name"| Server
    Server --> Bundle
    Server --> Chrome
    Chrome --> Web
    Server -->|"JSON or SSE"| Client

Each YAML file in tasks/ defines one endpoint. The server hot-reloads on every request — edit a task, call it again, no restart.

docs.title: Action reference
docs.text:
The complete vocabulary the flow interpreter understands. Every run: keyword is listed here with all of its parameters, defaults, output shape, and an example. This is the detailed companion to the condensed table in cookbook.md.

Each step in a task's flow: is one command:

- run: <action>            # the keyword (required)
  status: "Human text"     # optional: emitted as an SSE `status` event
  as: <name>               # optional: where the result is stored
  record: true             # optional: screencast just this step to a GIF
  params: { … }            # action-specific parameters
  do:                      # child steps, for the block actions
    - run: …

Conventions in the tables below:

  • A param written name?=default is optional with that default; name with no = is required (or the step errors).
  • "Output" describes what an as: binding receives, and is also what lands in the response data. Most artifact actions accept path: (write to disk), as: (return inline), or both.
  • Param values are templated first — see templating.md.
