docs.title: Sample 1 — Hello (basics/title)
docs.text: Complexity: minimal · Lines of DSL: 9 · Engine features: goto, extract

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Open example.com, return title and heading — equivalent to demo/tasks/basics/title.yaml.

docs.title: Capy source
docs.text:
grammar/samples/01-hello.capy:

task "basics/title"
    pool default
    timeout 15s
    transport rest

    status "Visiting example.com"
    goto "https://example.com"

    status "Reading title and heading"
    extract page from "html":
        title   text "title"
        heading text "h1"
        body    text "p"
    end
end

docs.title: Transpiled YAML
docs.text:
name: "basics/title"
poolTag: "default"
transports: ["rest"]
timeoutMs: 15000
flow:
  - status: "Visiting example.com"
    run: goto
    params: { url: "https://example.com" }
  - status: "Reading title and heading"
    run: extract
    as: page
    params:
      selector: "html"
      repeat: false
      fields:
        title:   { kind: text, selector: "title" }
        heading: { kind: text, selector: "h1" }
        body:    { kind: text, selector: "p" }

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
# Transpile
capy run capy/webtasks.capy tasks/basics/title.capy

# Execute (after Go integration or manual yaml drop-in)
executor call basics/title

docs.title: What Capy improved
docs.text:
YAML Capy 22 lines 9 lines Repeated run: / params: nesting Flat verbs kind: text boilerplate text "selector" sugar

Next: Sample 2 — Hacker News →

docs.title: Sample 2 — Hacker News crawl
docs.text: Complexity: low · Features: list extraction, wait-for, transforms

docs.title: Capy source
docs.text:
task "crawl/hackernews-top"
    pool default
    timeout 20s
    transport rest

    goto "https://news.ycombinator.com"
    wait-for "tr.athing" timeout 10s

    extract stories from "tr.athing" repeat:
        rank  text ".rank" trim
        title text ".titleline > a"
        url   attr href on ".titleline > a"
        site  text ".sitestr"
    end
end

docs.title: Compare to YAML
docs.text:
See demo/tasks/crawl/hackernews-top.yaml.

The Capy version keeps selectors visually aligned with field names — easier to scan in code review.

docs.title: Expected response
docs.text:
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "stories": [
      { "rank": "1.", "title": "…", "url": "https://…", "site": "…" }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
capy run capy/webtasks.capy tasks/crawl/hackernews-top.capy | diff - demo/tasks/crawl/hackernews-top.yaml

During migration, diff should be empty (modulo field ordering).

Next: Sample 3 — Search →

docs.title: Sample 3 — DuckDuckGo search
docs.text: Complexity: low · Features: input schema, URL templating

docs.title: Capy source
docs.text:
task "search/duckduckgo"
    pool default
    timeout 30s
    transport rest

    input q string required doc "Search query"

    goto "https://duckduckgo.com/?q={{q}}"
    wait-for "article[data-testid='result']" timeout 10s

    extract results from "article[data-testid='result']" repeat:
        title text "h2"
        link  attr href on "a"
    end
end

docs.title: Input binding
docs.text:
Caller:

executor call search/duckduckgo '{"q":"chromedp golang"}'

{{q}} in transpiled YAML resolves identically to hand-written tasks.

docs.title: Optional defaults
docs.text:
input q string default "webtasks" doc "Search query"

Or inline fallback in URL (still YAML templating):

goto "https://duckduckgo.com/?q={{q|or:webtasks}}"

Next: Sample 4 — Form fill →

docs.title: Sample 4 — Form fill (httpbin)
docs.text: Complexity: medium · Features: sendkeys, click, multi-input defaults

docs.title: Capy source
docs.text:
task "interaction/form-fill"
    pool default
    timeout 30s
    transport rest

    input custname  string default "Ada Lovelace"
    input custtel   string default "555-0100"
    input custemail string default "ada@example.com"
    input comments  string default "hi from webtasks"

    goto "https://httpbin.org/forms/post"
    wait-for "form input[name='custname']" timeout 10s

    sendkeys "input[name='custname']"  keys "{{custname}}"
    sendkeys "input[name='custtel']"   keys "{{custtel}}"
    sendkeys "input[name='custemail']" keys "{{custemail}}"
    sendkeys "textarea[name='comments']" keys "{{comments}}"

    click "form button"
    wait-for "pre" timeout 10s

    extract echoed from "html":
        body text "pre"
    end
end

docs.title: Why Capy helps
docs.text: Four similar sendkeys blocks in YAML differ only by selector and key name. In Capy they read as a uniform sequence — less vertical noise, easier for agents to append fields.

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
executor call interaction/form-fill
executor call interaction/form-fill '{"custname":"Grace","comments":"test"}'

Next: Sample 5 — SSE streaming →

docs.title: Sample 5 — SSE progress streaming
docs.text: Complexity: medium · Features: status, emit-event, dual transport

docs.title: Capy source
docs.text:
task "streaming/progress"
    pool default
    timeout 30s
    transport rest
    transport sse

    status "Step 1 of 4 — navigate"
    goto "https://example.com"
    emit progress "navigation complete" data { fraction: 0.25 }

    status "Step 2 of 4 — pause briefly"
    wait 1s
    emit progress "pause finished" data { fraction: 0.5 }

    status "Step 3 of 4 — read title"
    extract page from "html":
        title text "title"
    end
    emit progress "extracted title" data { fraction: 0.75 }

    status "Step 4 of 4 — done"
    wait 500
end

docs.title: SSE invocation
docs.text:
executor call streaming/progress '{}' true
curl -N -X POST localhost:8765/tasks/streaming/progress \
  -H 'Accept: text/event-stream' -d '{}'

docs.title: Capy vs YAML
docs.text:
Multiple transport lines replace YAML list syntax. emit progress … data {…} is clearer than nested emit-event params maps.

Next: Sample 6 — JS modules →

docs.title: Sample 6 — JS modules
docs.text: Complexity: medium · Features: fn: module reference, typed module paths

docs.title: Capy source
docs.text:
task "js-modules/page-stats"
    pool default
    timeout 20s
    transport rest

    input url string default "https://example.com"

    goto "{{url}}"
    wait-for "body" timeout 10s

    js stats fn "demo/page-stats.js" args []
end

Library validates demo/page-stats.js against type ModulePath.

docs.title: Inline vs module
docs.text:
Inline (small scripts)Module (reusable) 
js title script:
    return { title: document.title, links: document.links.length };
end

js stats fn "demo/page-stats.js" args ["{{selector}}"]

Modules stay in scripts/ — Capy does not inline JS; it only generates YAML pointing at bundle paths.

Next: Sample 7 — Control flow →

docs.title: Sample 7 — Control flow (call + loop)
docs.text: Complexity: high · Features: task composition, pagination loop

docs.title: call — reuse another task
docs.text:
task "control/call"
    pool default
    timeout 30s
    transport rest

    call "basics/title"
    return "{{page.title}}"
end

Equivalent to demo/tasks/control/call.yaml.

Restrict call targets with type ConcioTask or type TaskName enums for sandboxed agent bundles.

docs.title: loop — paginated crawl
docs.text:
task "crawl/quotes-paginated"
    pool default
    timeout 60s
    transport rest

    goto "https://quotes.toscrape.com"
    set all []

    loop max 10 while js "return document.querySelector('.next') !== null":
        extract page from "div.quote" repeat:
            text   text ".text"
            author text ".author"
            tags   text ".tag"
        end
        append all "{{page}}"
        click ".next"
        wait-for "div.quote" timeout 5s
    end

    return "{{all}}"
end

Note: set/append here are DSL statements transpiling to webtasks set actions — library defines them separately from inner-DSL set context.*.

docs.title: record-step debugging
docs.text:
record-step debug:
    wait-for ".slow-widget" timeout 30s
end

Transpiles to record: true on that step — GIF in $TMPDIR/webtasks-recordings/.

Next: Sample 8 — Rendering →

docs.title: Sample 8 — Rendering (PDF + screenshot)
docs.text: Complexity: medium · Features: artifacts, paths, base64 output

docs.title: PDF
docs.text:
task "rendering/pdf"
    pool default
    timeout 30s
    transport rest

    input url  string default "https://example.com"
    input path string default "/tmp/webtasks-demo/example.pdf"

    goto "{{url}}"
    wait-for "body" timeout 10s
    pdf pdf path "{{path}}" format A4 printBackground true
end

docs.title: Screenshot
docs.text:
task "basics/screenshot"
    pool default
    timeout 20s
    transport rest

    input url string default "https://example.com"

    goto "{{url}}"
    wait-for "body" timeout 10s
    screenshot png_b64 selector "."
end

docs.title: Full-page + dark mode
docs.text:
task "rendering/emulate-dark"
    pool default
    timeout 30s
    transport rest

    input url string default "https://example.com"

    goto "{{url}}"
    emulate dark
    screenshot shot selector "html" fullPage true
end

Capy groups rendering verbs in one visual cluster — easier than hunting through YAML keys (printBackground, format, etc.) without IDE schema support.

Next: Sample 9 — Concio bundle →

docs.title: Sample 9 — Concio production bundle
docs.text:
Complexity: expert · Features: secrets, persistent pool, blob capture, task chains

This sample sketches how the concio/ bundle would look as Capy tasks — not a full port, but the patterns that matter at scale.

docs.title: Bundle layout
docs.text:
concio/
├── capy/
│   └── webtasks.capy          # shared grammar (or extends base)
├── tasks/
│   ├── pool.capy              # OR keep pool.yaml
│   └── concio/
│       ├── setup.capy
│       ├── list-chats.capy
│       ├── get-messages.capy
│       └── capture-files.capy
├── scripts/concio/*.js        # unchanged
├── secrets.yaml               # unchanged
└── static-mounts.yaml

docs.title: setup.capy — idempotent login
docs.text:
task "concio/setup"
    pool concio
    timeout 120s
    transport rest

    goto "https://app.example.com/login"
    js logged_in fn "concio/check-logged-in.js"

    # if not logged_in — library emits conditional via inner DSL
    # (or separate branch tasks; keep setup idempotent)

    js _ fn "concio/navigate-login.js"
    js _ fn "concio/login.js"          # uses {{CONCIO_PASSWORD}} from secrets
    wait-for ".chat-sidebar" timeout 30s
end

Secrets still resolve via secrets.yaml + env — Capy only authors the flow.

docs.title: get-messages.capy — scroll + extract
docs.text:
task "concio/get-messages"
    pool concio
    timeout 300s
    transport rest
    setup concio/setup

    input peerName string required doc "Chat display name"

    js _ fn "concio/open-chat-by-name.js" args ["{{peerName}}"]

    scroll-until-stable ".chat-panel" up stable 1000ms max 50

    js messages fn "concio/dump-chat-panel.js"
    return "{{messages}}"
end

docs.title: capture-files.capy — blob hook pattern
docs.text:
task "concio/capture-files"
    pool concio
    timeout 600s
    transport rest
    setup concio/setup

    js _ fn "concio/install-download-hook.js"

    capture-network captures:
        js _ fn "concio/click-all-files.js"
        js files fn "concio/poll-captures.js"
    end

    return "{{files}}"
end

docs.title: Why Capy at this scale
docs.text: Concern Without Capy With Capy 10+ tasks sharing login Copy-paste setup prelude setup concio/setup one line JS module paths String typos in YAML type ModulePath validation Agent-authored tasks Can invent dangerous steps Enum-limited call targets Onboarding Read 780-line actions.md capy docs webtasks.capy

docs.title: Orchestration outside webtasks
docs.text:
The Python client concio_extract still drives per-chat sweeps. Capy tasks remain HTTP endpoints — only the file format changed.

docs.title: Demo catalogue
docs.text: The demo/ bundle contains 38 runnable tasks across 11 categories. Each task is a self-contained YAML file you can read in under a minute.

docs.title: Run all demos
docs.text:
# Start the server with the demo bundle
WEBTASKS_BUNDLE=$(pwd)/demo ./build/webtasks &

# List everything
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8765/tasks | python3 -m json.tool

# Run any task by name
curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/tasks/basics/title \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{}'

With the executor helper:

executor server &          # defaults WEBTASKS_BUNDLE to ./demo
executor list-tasks
executor call basics/title
executor call crawl/hackernews-top
executor call streaming/progress '{}' true   # SSE stream

docs.title: Suggested learning path
docs.text:
Follow this order if you're new to webtasks:

  1. Basics → title — smallest possible task
  2. Basics → screenshot — capture a PNG
  3. Crawl → hackernews-top — list extraction
  4. Search → duckduckgo — input templating
  5. Interaction → form-fill — typing and clicking
  6. Streaming → progress — live SSE events
  7. Control → call — compose tasks
  8. Recording → record — GIF screencast

docs.title: Hot-reload
docs.text:
The server reloads YAML from the bundle on every request. Edit any file under demo/tasks/, then immediately re-run — no restart needed.

The demo pool is configured for 3 concurrent windows so several tasks can run in parallel:

# demo/tasks/pool.yaml
pools:
  default: { size: 3 }

docs.title: Add your own demo
docs.text:
cp demo/tasks/basics/title.yaml demo/tasks/basics/my-task.yaml
# edit name + flow
executor call basics/my-task

Action vocabulary: Actions reference · Cookbook

docs.title: Backend & export demos
docs.text: Four tasks for outbound HTTP (no browser) and structured data export.

docs.title: http-get
docs.text:
Fetch a URL via Go's HTTP client — no Chrome window needed.

executor call backend/http-get
executor call backend/http-get '{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/chromedp/chromedp"}'

Concepts: http-get, mixing browser and non-browser steps in one bundle.

Use when you've discovered a JSON API via Network → capture.

docs.title: http-post
docs.text:
POST JSON to an endpoint and return the response.

executor call backend/http-post
Pattern 
- run: http-post
  as: response
  params:
    url: "https://httpbin.org/post"
    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" }
    body: '{"key": "{{value}}"}'

Concepts: webhook triggers, API integration without browser overhead.

docs.title: export-csv
docs.text:
Extract data and export as CSV.

executor call backend/export-csv

Concepts: export action with format: csv, server-side file generation.

docs.title: export-formats
docs.text:
Export the same data as CSV, NDJSON, and Markdown in one task.

executor call backend/export-formats
Export action 
- run: export
  params:
    format: "ndjson"    # csv | ndjson | markdown
    path: "/tmp/out.ndjson"
    data: "{{items}}"

Concepts: multiple export formats, piping extract results to files.

docs.title: Browser vs backend steps
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph browser["Needs Chrome"]
        goto
        extract
        click
    end
    subgraph backend["Go HTTP client"]
        http-get
        http-post
        export
    end

    browser -->|"DOM data"| backend
Step type Pool lease? Speed Browser (goto, extract, …) Yes Slower Backend (http-get, export) No* Fast

*Backend steps still run inside a task that may hold a window — structure tasks to minimize browser time.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Crawl — extract data to export
  • Cookbook §11 — ship exports in production

docs.title: Basics demos
docs.text: Five minimal tasks that teach the core building blocks: navigate, wait, extract, screenshot, and inline JavaScript.

docs.title: title
docs.text:
The smallest possible task — open example.com and return the page title.

executor call basics/title
Task YAMLResponse 
name: "basics/title"
poolTag: "default"
transports: ["rest"]
timeoutMs: 15000

flow:
  - status: "Visiting example.com"
    run: goto
    params: { url: "https://example.com" }

  - status: "Reading title and heading"
    run: extract
    as: page
    params:
      selector: "html"
      repeat: false
      fields:
        title:   { kind: text, selector: "title" }
        heading: { kind: text, selector: "h1" }
        body:    { kind: text, selector: "p" }

{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "page": {
      "title": "Example Domain",
      "heading": "Example Domain",
      "body": "This domain is for use in documentation examples …"
    }
  }
}

Concepts: goto, extract, as: naming, status: lines.

docs.title: screenshot
docs.text:
Capture a viewport PNG of any URL. Returns base64 under data.png_b64.

executor call basics/screenshot
executor call basics/screenshot '{"url":"https://news.ycombinator.com"}'
Task YAMLSave to disk 
input:
  url: { type: string, required: false, default: "https://example.com" }

flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "{{url}}" }
  - run: wait-for
    params: { selector: "body", timeoutMs: 10000 }
  - run: screenshot
    as: png_b64
    params: { selector: "." }

curl -s -X POST localhost:8765/tasks/basics/screenshot \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"url":"https://example.com"}' \
  | python3 -c '
import json, sys, base64
b = json.load(sys.stdin)["data"]["png_b64"]
open("/tmp/shot.png", "wb").write(base64.b64decode(b))
print("saved /tmp/shot.png")
'

Concepts: input schema, {{url}} templating, screenshot action.

docs.title: inline-js
docs.text:
Run arbitrary JavaScript inline with templated arguments.

executor call basics/inline-js

The task uses a script: block to read document.title and return structured data without a separate .js file.

Concepts: run: js, inline script: blocks, returning values from JS.

See also: JS modules for reusable scripts in scripts/.

docs.title: save-html
docs.text:
Dump the fully rendered HTML to a server-side path.

executor call basics/save-html

Concepts: write-files, server-side file output, rendered DOM vs source.

docs.title: wait-then-click
docs.text:
Navigate, wait for an element, click it, then extract the result.

executor call basics/wait-then-click
Flow pattern 
flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "…" }
  - run: wait-for
    params: { selector: "button.submit", timeoutMs: 10000 }
  - run: action
    params: { action: click, selector: "button.submit" }
  - run: extract
    as: result
    params: { … }

Concepts: wait-for, action(click), chaining steps.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Crawl demos — extract lists from real websites
  • Build your own task — author from scratch

docs.title: Real-world: Concio bundle
docs.text:
A production-grade config bundle for scraping a logged-in Concio (Starise IM) account — demonstrates secrets, persistent sessions, JS modules, blob capture, and multi-task orchestration.

The Concio bundle lives separately from demo/ at concio/.

docs.title: What it produces
docs.text:
<owner>/
├── chats/<YYYY>/<MM>/<DD>/
│   └── chat_<owner>_<peer>_<ts>_<mo|mt>_<msgIdHi>_<msgIdLo>.json
├── files/                       # decrypted attachment bytes
└── users_mapping.json
groups_mapping.json

Designed for consumption by rag-ingestion / message-reader pipelines.

docs.title: Bundle layout
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph bundle["concio/"]
        tasks["tasks/concio/*.yaml"]
        scripts["scripts/concio/*.js"]
        secrets["secrets.yaml"]
        mounts["static-mounts.yaml"]
    end

    subgraph tasks_detail["Tasks"]
        setup[setup.yaml]
        list[list-chats.yaml]
        msgs[get-messages.yaml]
        files[capture-files.yaml]
    end

    tasks --> tasks_detail
File Purpose tasks/concio/setup.yaml Idempotent login tasks/concio/list-chats.yaml Sidebar chat list tasks/concio/list-contacts.yaml Contacts directory tasks/concio/list-groups.yaml Groups directory tasks/concio/get-messages.yaml Open chat + scroll + extract tasks/concio/capture-files.yaml Encrypted attachment capture scripts/concio/login.js Form fill with {{CONCIO_PASSWORD}} scripts/concio/install-download-hook.js Blob capture patch secrets.yaml Declares CONCIO_PASSWORD

docs.title: Run it
docs.text:
# 1. Start with the concio bundle
WEBTASKS_BUNDLE=$(pwd)/concio ./build/webtasks &
# Resolve CONCIO_PASSWORD via env, --flag, or prompt (see secrets.yaml)

# 2. Log in (idempotent)
executor call concio/setup

# 3. Explore
executor call concio/list-chats
executor call concio/list-contacts

# 4. Pull one chat's messages
executor call concio/get-messages '{"peerName":"Alice"}'

Or use the all-in-one helper:

executor concio-extract

docs.title: Declared secrets
docs.text:
# concio/secrets.yaml
secrets:
  - name: CONCIO_PASSWORD
    description: "Concio account password"
    required: true
    sensitive: true
    sources: ["env", "arg", "prompt"]

Used in JS as {{CONCIO_PASSWORD}} after resolution at startup.

→ Secrets reference

docs.title: Persistent login profile
docs.text:
The concio pool uses a persistent Chrome profile so login survives restarts:

# concio/tasks/pool.yaml
pools:
  concio: { size: 1, persistent: true }

→ Pools reference

docs.title: Blob download hook
docs.text:
Apps that decrypt client-side before download can't use normal click-and-poll. The bundle patches URL.createObjectURL to capture blobs:

// scripts/concio/install-download-hook.js (conceptual)
// Intercepts blob URLs and posts captured bytes back to webtasks

→ Cookbook §7

docs.title: Scroll-to-load history
docs.text:
- run: scroll-until-stable
  params:
    selector: ".chat-panel"
    direction: up
    stableMs: 1000
    maxIterations: 50

Same primitive as Interaction → scroll-feed.

docs.title: Static mounts for captured files
docs.text:
# concio/static-mounts.yaml
mounts:
  - prefix: /files
    path: "${CONCIO_OUTPUT_DIR}/files"

Serve captured attachments over HTTP for downstream processing.

→ Static mounts

docs.title: Task chain
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant S as webtasks
    participant C as Concio web app

    Op->>S: concio/setup
    S->>C: login (if needed)
    Op->>S: concio/list-chats
    S->>C: read sidebar
    S-->>Op: chat names + ids
    Op->>S: concio/get-messages {"peerName":"…"}
    S->>C: open chat, scroll up, extract
    S-->>Op: message JSON
    Op->>S: concio/capture-files
    S->>C: click attachments, capture blobs
    S-->>Op: file bytes

docs.title: When to copy this pattern
docs.text:
Use the Concio bundle as a template when your target:

  • Requires login (persistent profile + setup task)
  • Uses client-side encryption before download (blob hook)
  • Has infinite-scroll history (scroll-until-stable)
  • Needs multiple coordinated tasks (call chains)

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Secrets — declare and resolve credentials
  • Pools — persistent profiles and concurrency
  • Build your own task — start from scratch

docs.title: Control flow demos
docs.text: Five tasks demonstrating task composition, loops, JS conditions, and nested recording.

docs.title: call
docs.text:
Run another task's flow inside the current window, then use its results.

executor call control/call
Task YAML 
name: "control/call"
flow:
  - run: call
    params: { task: "basics/title" }
  - run: return
    params: { value: "{{page.title}}" }

flowchart TD
    A["control/call starts"]
    B["call basics/title\n(reuses same window)"]
    C["page.title available\nin template context"]
    D["return title string"]

    A --> B --> C --> D

Concepts: call action, task reuse, template context from called task.

Build libraries of small tasks and compose them — the Concio bundle uses this heavily (setup → list-chats → get-messages).

docs.title: loop
docs.text:
Repeat steps while a condition holds — iterate over pages, retry on failure.

executor call control/loop
Pattern 
- run: loop
  params:
    maxIterations: 10
  while:
    run: js
    params:
      script: "return document.querySelector('.next-page') !== null"
  do:
    - run: extract
      as: page_items
      params: { … }
    - run: action
      params: { action: click, selector: ".next-page" }

Concepts: loop with while: condition, do: body, pagination.

See also Crawl → quotes-paginated.

docs.title: loop-fn
docs.text:
Same as loop but the condition comes from a JS module.

executor call control/loop-fn

Concepts: fn: in loop conditions, cleaner complex logic.

docs.title: await-js
docs.text:
Block until a JavaScript expression returns true.

executor call control/await-js
Use case 
- run: await-js
  params:
    script: "return document.querySelector('.loaded') !== null"
    timeoutMs: 30000
    pollMs: 500

Concepts: waiting for JS-driven UI state, SPAs, lazy widgets.

docs.title: record-step
docs.text:
Record a GIF of a single step (not the whole flow).

executor call control/record-step

Concepts: combining Recording with granular step control.

docs.title: Control flow cheat sheet
docs.text:
Action Purpose call Run another task inline loop Repeat while condition true await-js Poll until JS returns true set Bind a variable return Early exit with value for-each Iterate over an array

Full reference: Actions

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Recording — visual capture of flows
  • Concio — production task chains

docs.title: Crawl & scrape demos
docs.text: Five tasks that extract structured lists from live websites — the patterns you'll use for most scraping workloads.

docs.title: hackernews-top
docs.text:
Front-page Hacker News: rank, title, URL, and site for every story.

executor call crawl/hackernews-top
Task YAMLResponse shape 
name: "crawl/hackernews-top"
poolTag: "default"
transports: ["rest"]
timeoutMs: 20000

flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "https://news.ycombinator.com" }

  - run: wait-for
    params: { selector: "tr.athing", timeoutMs: 10000 }

  - run: extract
    as: stories
    params:
      selector: "tr.athing"
      repeat: true
      fields:
        rank:  { kind: text, selector: ".rank",        transform: trim }
        title: { kind: text, selector: ".titleline > a" }
        url:   { kind: attr, selector: ".titleline > a", name: "href" }
        site:  { kind: text, selector: ".sitestr" }

{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "stories": [
      { "rank": "1.", "title": "…", "url": "https://…", "site": "…" },
      …
    ]
  }
}

sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as webtasks
    participant HN as news.ycombinator.com

    C->>S: POST /tasks/crawl/hackernews-top
    S->>HN: goto
    S->>HN: wait-for tr.athing
    S->>HN: extract (repeat: true)
    S-->>C: JSON array of stories

Concepts: extract with repeat: true, CSS selectors, kind: attr.

docs.title: github-trending
docs.text:
Trending repositories with language and time-period inputs.

executor call crawl/github-trending
executor call crawl/github-trending '{"language":"go","since":"weekly"}'
Input schemaTemplated URL 
input:
  language: { type: string, required: false, default: "" }
  since:    { type: string, required: false, default: "daily" }

- run: goto
  params: { url: "https://github.com/trending/{{language}}?since={{since}}" }

Extracts: slug, href, description, language, stars, forks.

Concepts: optional inputs with defaults, URL path templating.

docs.title: wikipedia-toc
docs.text:
Wikipedia table of contents — mixes single-object and repeated extraction in one task.

executor call crawl/wikipedia-toc

Concepts: repeat: false for one record, repeat: true for a list in the same flow.

docs.title: trending-papers
docs.text:
The canonical smoke test — 100 trending papers from Hugging Face.

executor call crawl/trending-papers

This is the same task shipped in bundle-example/ as examples/trending-papers. Use it to verify a fresh deployment:

curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8765/health
executor call crawl/trending-papers
# expect ~100 papers with title + href

Concepts: production smoke test, complex selector lists.

docs.title: quotes-paginated
docs.text:
Multi-page scraping pattern against quotes.toscrape.com.

executor call crawl/quotes-paginated

Concepts: pagination loops, following "next" links — a pattern you'll adapt for any paginated site.

See Control flow → loop for looping constructs.

docs.title: Selector tips
docs.text:
Goal Pattern All table rows tr.athing, article.row Field inside row .titleline > a Attribute kind: attr, name: href Trim whitespace transform: trim

docs.title: Downloads demo
docs.text: Native browser download capture — click a link and poll for the file bytes.

docs.title: grab-image
docs.text:
Click a download link and capture the PNG bytes.

executor call downloads/grab-image
Flow pattern 
flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "https://example.com/page-with-download" }
  - run: download-each
    as: files
    params:
      selector: "a.download-link"
      timeoutMs: 30000

Concepts: download-each, native CDP download behavior (no WebDriver hacks).

sequenceDiagram
    participant S as webtasks
    participant C as Chrome
    participant FS as Download dir

    S->>C: Browser.setDownloadBehavior
    S->>C: click download link
    C->>FS: file bytes written
    S->>FS: poll until complete
    S-->>S: return base64 + metadata

docs.title: Download strategies
docs.text:
webtasks supports two download paths:

Method When to use download-each Normal <a download> or Content-Disposition links Blob hook (js) Apps that decrypt client-side before download (see Concio)

The Concio bundle uses a URL.createObjectURL patch to capture encrypted blobs — see Concio → capture-files.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Downloads land in WEBTASKS_DOWNLOADS_DIR (default: system temp). Each window gets an isolated subdirectory.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Rendering — PDF and screenshot capture
  • Static mounts — serve captured files over HTTP

docs.title: Interaction demos
docs.text: Tasks that interact with pages — filling forms, clicking buttons, and scrolling infinite feeds.

docs.title: form-fill
docs.text:
Fill the httpbin.org POST form and return what the server echoed back.

executor call interaction/form-fill
executor call interaction/form-fill '{"custname":"Ada","custtel":"555","custemail":"a@b","comments":"hi"}'
Task YAML (abbreviated) 
input:
  custname:  { type: string, required: false, default: "Ada Lovelace" }
  custtel:   { type: string, required: false, default: "555-0100" }
  custemail: { type: string, required: false, default: "ada@example.com" }
  comments:  { type: string, required: false, default: "hi from webtasks" }

flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "https://httpbin.org/forms/post" }
  - run: wait-for
    params: { selector: "form input[name='custname']", timeoutMs: 10000 }
  - run: sendkeys
    params: { selector: "input[name='custname']",  keys: "{{custname}}" }
  - run: sendkeys
    params: { selector: "input[name='custtel']",   keys: "{{custtel}}" }
  - run: sendkeys
    params: { selector: "input[name='custemail']", keys: "{{custemail}}" }
  - run: sendkeys
    params: { selector: "textarea[name='comments']", keys: "{{comments}}" }
  - run: action
    params: { action: click, selector: "form button" }
  - run: wait-for
    params: { selector: "pre", timeoutMs: 10000 }
  - run: extract
    as: echoed
    params:
      selector: "html"
      repeat: false
      fields:
        body: { kind: text, selector: "pre" }

flowchart TD
    A[goto form page] --> B[wait-for inputs]
    B --> C[sendkeys × 4 fields]
    C --> D[click submit]
    D --> E[wait-for response]
    E --> F[extract echoed JSON]

Concepts: sendkeys, action(click), form selectors by name=, waiting for navigation results.

Debugging selectors

Run with WEBTASKS_HEADLESS=false and watch Chrome fill the form live.

docs.title: scroll-feed
docs.text:
Scroll an infinite-scroll demo page until the DOM stabilizes.

executor call interaction/scroll-feed
Key step 
- run: scroll-until-stable
  params:
    selector: "body"
    direction: down
    stableMs: 800
    maxIterations: 20

Concepts: scroll-until-stable, loading lazy content, chat-history patterns.

This is the same primitive used in production bundles like Concio → get-messages to load full chat history.

docs.title: Action reference (interaction)
docs.text:
Action Purpose sendkeys Type into an input (selector + keys) action click, double-click, hover, etc. wait-for Block until selector appears scroll-until-stable Scroll until DOM stops changing wait Fixed delay (duration in ms)

Full list: Actions reference

docs.text:

  • Concio — scroll-to-top on a real chat app

docs.title: JS modules demos
docs.text: Three tasks that load reusable JavaScript from scripts/demo/*.js instead of inline script: blocks.

docs.title: Why JS modules?
docs.text:
Inline script: blocks get unreadable past a few lines. Drop scripts into bundle/scripts/ and reference them with fn::

- run: js
  as: result
  params:
    fn: "demo/page-stats.js"
    args: ["{{url}}"]

The server loads scripts/demo/page-stats.js from the bundle and executes it in the browser context.

flowchart LR
    YAML["Task YAML\nfn: demo/foo.js"]
    Bundle["scripts/demo/foo.js"]
    Chrome["Chrome V8"]
    Result["JSON result"]

    YAML --> Bundle --> Chrome --> Result

docs.title: meta-tags
docs.text:
Extract Open Graph and meta tags from any page.

executor call js-modules/meta-tags
executor call js-modules/meta-tags '{"url":"https://github.com/chromedp/chromedp"}'

Script: demo/scripts/demo/get-meta-tags.js

Concepts: one task ↔ one JS module, passing the current page DOM to JS.

docs.title: page-stats
docs.text:
DOM summary helper — counts elements, links, images, etc.

executor call js-modules/page-stats

Script: demo/scripts/demo/page-stats.js

Script pattern 
// scripts/demo/page-stats.js
// arguments[0] = optional override selector root
return {
  links: document.querySelectorAll("a").length,
  images: document.querySelectorAll("img").length,
  headings: document.querySelectorAll("h1,h2,h3").length,
};

Concepts: returning structured objects from JS, reusable helpers.

docs.title: all-links
docs.text:
Collect every link on a page with text and href.

executor call js-modules/all-links
executor call js-modules/all-links '{"maxLinks":"50"}'

Script: demo/scripts/demo/all-links.js

Concepts: passing args from YAML into JS via args: array.

docs.title: Inline vs module
docs.text:
Inline script: fn: module Good for 1–5 lines Good for anything longer Lives in the YAML Lives in scripts/ Hard to test/format Normal .js file No reuse Shared across tasks

Cookbook recipe: §4 Use a JS module

docs.title: Network demos
docs.text: Four tasks for observing browser network activity, cookies, console output, and network idle detection.

docs.title: capture
docs.text:
Record every network request while a page loads — returns a HAR-ish summary.

executor call network/capture
executor call network/capture '{"url":"https://quotes.toscrape.com"}'
Task YAML 
flow:
  - run: capture-network
    as: har
    do:
      - run: goto
        params: { url: "{{url}}" }
      - run: wait-for-network-idle
        params: { idleMs: 800, timeoutMs: 20000 }

flowchart TD
    Start[capture-network starts recording]
    Do["do: sub-steps run"]
    Goto[goto URL]
    Idle[wait-for-network-idle]
    Stop[return HAR summary under data.har]

    Start --> Do
    Do --> Goto --> Idle
    Do --> Stop

Concepts: capture-network wrapper with do: block, HAR-style output.

Use this to discover XHR endpoints before switching to Backend → http-get.

docs.title: cookies
docs.text:
Read and write browser cookies for the current session.

executor call network/cookies

Concepts: session persistence, cookie jar inspection, auth debugging.

Pairs with Pools → persistent profiles for login sessions that survive restarts.

docs.title: console
docs.text:
Capture browser console log lines during a flow.

executor call network/console

Concepts: capture-console, debugging JS errors on target pages.

docs.title: idle
docs.text:
Wait until network activity settles — useful before extraction on SPAs.

executor call network/idle
Key params 
- run: wait-for-network-idle
  params:
    idleMs: 800        # ms of silence required
    timeoutMs: 20000   # give up after this

Concepts: SPA loading patterns, when wait-for selector isn't enough.

docs.title: Debugging workflow
docs.text:
1. Run network/capture on the target URL
  2. Inspect data.har for XHR/fetch endpoints
  3. If an API is available, switch to http-get (no browser needed)
  4. If DOM rendering is required, use wait-for-network-idle before extract

docs.text:

  • Secrets — auth tokens for authenticated requests

docs.title: Recording demo
docs.text: Capture a browser flow as an animated GIF (or MP4 with ffmpeg).

docs.title: record
docs.text:
Navigate to a page, scroll it, and save an animated GIF of the action.

executor call recording/record
executor call recording/record '{"url":"https://quotes.toscrape.com","path":"/tmp/demo.gif"}'
Task YAML 
name: "recording/record"
input:
  url:    { type: string, default: "https://quotes.toscrape.com" }
  path:   { type: string, default: "/tmp/webtasks-demo/flow.gif" }
  format: { type: string, default: "gif", doc: "gif or mp4" }

flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "{{url}}" }
  - run: record
    as: clip
    params:
      format: "{{format}}"
      path: "{{path}}"
      fps: 4
    do:
      - run: scroll-until-stable
        params:
          selector: "body"
          direction: down
          stableMs: 800
          maxIterations: 8
      - run: wait
        params: { duration: "500" }

flowchart LR
    Goto[goto page] --> Rec["record (fps: 4)"]
    Rec --> Scroll[scroll-until-stable]
    Scroll --> Wait[wait 500ms]
    Wait --> GIF["GIF at path\n+ base64 in data.clip"]

docs.title: Output formats
docs.text:
Format Requirement Use case gif Pure Go (built-in) Docs, Slack, README embeds mp4 ffmpeg on PATH Higher quality, video pipelines 
executor call recording/record '{"format":"mp4","path":"/tmp/demo.mp4"}'

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Param Default Description fps 4 Frames per second path (required) Server-side output path format gif gif or mp4 do: — Steps to perform while recording

The do: block defines what happens during the recording window — only those steps appear in the animation.

docs.title: Single-step recording
docs.text: For recording just one action, see Control → record-step.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
Lower fps for smaller files

fps: 2 is often enough for scroll demos. Use fps: 8 for click interactions.

Headful recording

WEBTASKS_HEADLESS=false lets you watch what's being captured live.

docs.title: Rendering demos
docs.text: Five tasks for capturing pages as PDF, PNG, MHTML, and emulated device views.

docs.title: pdf
docs.text:
Render a page to PDF via Chrome's print engine.

executor call rendering/pdf
executor call rendering/pdf '{"url":"https://news.ycombinator.com","path":"/tmp/hn.pdf"}'
Task YAML 
input:
  url:  { type: string, default: "https://example.com" }
  path: { type: string, default: "/tmp/webtasks-demo/example.pdf" }

flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "{{url}}" }
  - run: wait-for
    params: { selector: "body", timeoutMs: 10000 }
  - run: pdf
    as: pdf
    params:
      path: "{{path}}"
      format: "A4"
      printBackground: true

Returns both a server-side file at path and base64 in data.pdf.

Concepts: pdf action, printBackground, dual output (path + as).

executor call rendering/snapshot

executor call rendering/fullpage-shot

executor call rendering/html-to-pdf

executor call rendering/emulate-dark

• Recording — animated GIF instead of static capture
  • Network → capture — HAR alongside rendering

docs.title: Search demos
docs.text: Two tasks showing how caller input drives URLs and how to combine browser automation with JSON APIs.

docs.title: duckduckgo
docs.text:
Search DuckDuckGo with a query string from the caller.

executor call search/duckduckgo '{"q":"chromedp golang"}'
executor call search/duckduckgo '{"q":"webtasks yaml automation"}'
Input + templated URL 
input:
  q:
    type: string
    required: true
    doc: "Search query"

flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "https://duckduckgo.com/?q={{q}}" }
  - run: wait-for
    params: { selector: "article[data-testid='result']", timeoutMs: 10000 }
  - run: extract
    as: results
    params:
      selector: "article[data-testid='result']"
      repeat: true
      fields:
        title: { kind: text, selector: "h2" }
        link:  { kind: attr, selector: "a", name: "href" }

flowchart LR
    Input["Caller JSON\n{\"q\": \"…\"}"]
    Template["URL template\n?q={{q}}"]
    Browser["Chrome navigates"]
    Extract["extract repeat:true"]
    Output["results array"]

    Input --> Template --> Browser --> Extract --> Output

Concepts: required inputs, URL encoding via templating, result extraction.

URL encoding

For queries with spaces or special characters, the templating engine passes values as-is. Encode in the caller if needed, or use a js step to encodeURIComponent().

docs.title: hn-search
docs.text:
Hacker News search via the Algolia JSON API — no browser scraping needed for the search itself, but uses js for post-processing.

executor call search/hn-search '{"q":"go concurrency"}'

This demo shows the hybrid pattern:

  1. goto an API URL or use http-get for pure HTTP
  2. js to parse and reshape the JSON response
  3. return structured results

Concepts: API-driven tasks, JS post-processing, when not to scrape the DOM.

Compare with Backend → http-get for outbound HTTP without a browser window.

docs.title: Pattern: input → URL → extract
docs.text:
Most search/scrape tasks follow this template:

input:
  q: { type: string, required: true }

flow:
  - run: goto
    params: { url: "https://example.com/search?q={{q}}" }
  - run: wait-for
    params: { selector: ".results", timeoutMs: 10000 }
  - run: extract
    as: results
    params:
      selector: ".result-item"
      repeat: true
      fields: { … }

Copy this skeleton for any search endpoint.

docs.title: Streaming (SSE) demo
docs.text: One task that demonstrates live progress via Server-Sent Events.

docs.title: progress
docs.text:
A four-step flow that emits status, progress, and done events as it runs.

# Sync REST (waits for final JSON)
executor call streaming/progress

# SSE stream (live events)
executor call streaming/progress '{}' true
curl SSEPython SSE 
curl -N -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8765/tasks/streaming/progress \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Accept: text/event-stream' \
  -d '{}'

import requests

with requests.post(
    "http://127.0.0.1:8765/tasks/streaming/progress",
    json={},
    headers={"Accept": "text/event-stream"},
    stream=True,
) as resp:
    for line in resp.iter_lines(decode_unicode=True):
        if line:
            print(line)

docs.title: Event stream
docs.text:
Sample output:

event: status
data: {"text":"Step 1 of 4 — navigate","data":null}

event: progress
data: {"text":"navigation complete","data":{"fraction":0.25}}

event: status
data: {"text":"Step 2 of 4 — pause briefly","data":null}

event: progress
data: {"text":"pause finished","data":{"fraction":0.5}}

event: status
data: {"text":"Step 3 of 4 — read title","data":null}

event: progress
data: {"text":"extracted title","data":{"fraction":0.75}}

event: status
data: {"text":"Step 4 of 4 — done","data":null}

event: done
data: {"ok":true,"data":{"page":{"title":"Example Domain"}}}

docs.title: Task YAML
docs.text:
name: "streaming/progress"
poolTag: "default"
transports: ["rest", "sse"]
timeoutMs: 30000

flow:
  - status: "Step 1 of 4 — navigate"
    run: goto
    params: { url: "https://example.com" }

  - run: emit-event
    params:
      kind: "progress"
      text: "navigation complete"
      data: { fraction: 0.25 }

  - status: "Step 2 of 4 — pause briefly"
    run: wait
    params: { duration: "1_000" }

  - run: emit-event
    params:
      kind: "progress"
      text: "pause finished"
      data: { fraction: 0.5 }

  # … steps 3–4 …

Concepts:

  • transports: ["rest", "sse"] — opt in to streaming
  • Every status: line becomes an SSE status event automatically
  • emit-event sends custom progress (or any kind) events with arbitrary JSON
  • Final result arrives as event: done

docs.title: When to use SSE
docs.text:
Use SSE when… Use sync REST when… Long-running tasks (minutes) Tasks finish in seconds UI shows a progress bar Batch/cron jobs User waits interactively Fire-and-forget with polling

Protocol details: HTTP API → SSE
